explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF; leaves row-count chatter enabled, increasing client and network noise.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF; allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * from Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column of the table.
SELECT TOP (10) returns an arbitrary set of rows because no ordering is specified.
DISTINCT used on OrderID which is already guaranteed unique as a primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR() applied directly to the indexed OrderDate column prevents a simple range seek.
Comparing int column CustomerID with nvarchar input @CustomerIdTex can force conversion.
OR-based optional filtering on @CustomerID makes cardinality estimation and seeking difficult.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN subquery can produce NULL results which leads to surprising behavior.
Wrapping CustomerID in ISNULL() makes the predicate non-sargable.
Arithmetic operation OrderID + 0 prevents direct index matching.
Inequality operator <> scans broad portions of the table.
CROSS JOIN omits a join predicate and multiplies Orders by Customers.
Comma-separated table list in FROM clause obscures join predicates.
Correlated scalar subquery executes a lookup for every qualifying order row.
Correlated EXISTS check per outer row could be expressed as a set join.
The same CASE expression is repeated in both the select list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a column position which is fragile if the select list changes.
OFFSET pagination lacks an explicit, stable tie-breaker in the ORDER BY clause.
UNION used between branches that are already disjoint by construction.
COUNT(*) > 0 is used when only existence of rows is needed.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
Use of a table variable to hold non-trivial amounts of data may result in poor cardinality estimates.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates a temp-table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
Uses a cursor for work that can be expressed set-wise.
WHILE loop processes rows one at a time instead of using a set-based operation.
Building SQL string by concatenating variables into executable SQL text creates an injection risk.
EXEC used without parameterized inputs creates a security risk.
Building SQL string by concatenating variables into executable SQL text creates an injection risk.
sp_executesql used without parameterized inputs creates a security risk.
Explicitly created temporary table #Scratch is not dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
Uses RAISERROR rather than THROW for new error paths.
Use of PRINT for diagnostics is unsuitable as it is buffered.
Embeds a business identifier (CustomerID = 1) directly in SQL.
Embeds an unlabelled date constant '20160101' in query logic.
Relies on session-sensitive date string conversion without an explicit style parameter.
COALESCE used in a join predicate inhibits normal join optimization.
Self-joins Orders solely to retrieve the same key already available in the row.
Sorting by an unselective text column (Comments) can be slow.
Aggregates the entire fact-like order-line table without a restrictive filter.
Row-level predicate ol.OrderID > 0 placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
ORDER BY on a query guaranteed to return one row (COUNT(*)) is unnecessary.
Use of MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
UPDATE statement sets Comments = Comments which evaluates to the current value.
DELETE based on a broad business value (Quantity = -1) rather than a stable key.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION not wrapped in a BEGIN TRY ... BEGIN CATCH block.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
Invokes a system routine (sp_who) without a schema qualifier.
Changing session setting (SET ROWCOUNT 1) and not restoring it.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on CustomerName.
Building SQL string by concatenating variables into executable SQL text creates an injection risk.
sp_executesql used without parameterized inputs creates a security risk.
UPDATE statement lacks a WHERE clause.
Embeds an unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' in query logic.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL instead of IS NULL.
Scalar user-defined function dbo.GetCustomerType called in a SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation.
CATCH block neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
Explicit table/index hint WITH (INDEX = 1) used in production code.
Excessively nested subqueries (3+ levels) should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti copies every current and future column of the result set.
